ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'chicken rice dish' class; no specific soya sauce chicken rice entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated soya sauce chicken rice code. Closest defensible match is braised chicken meat dish (A0CKM). Rice component is implicit in the dish classification.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for soya sauce chicken rice as a composite dish. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard hawker-centre serving of soya sauce chicken rice consists of approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of braised chicken pieces (roughly 150–180 g) served over steamed jasmine rice (approximately 150–170 g), typically accompanied by a small amount of braising sauce drizzled over the top.

nutrient_source.title: Soya Sauce Chicken Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database lists soya sauce chicken rice as a composite dish. Per-100g values for the full dish (chicken + rice + sauce) are drawn from HPB records and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for braised chicken (USDA #05064) and cooked jasmine rice (USDA #20444) to validate macro plausibility. Micronutrient values are consistent with HPB-reported ranges for similar Cantonese braised chicken rice dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy braised chicken data (approximately 3.5 g per 100 g of composite dish).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for braised soy chicken rice based on composite dish GI methodology
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for soya sauce chicken rice as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate derived from: (1) the GI of plain steamed white rice (~72, University of Sydney GI Database), (2) the low GI contribution of the braised chicken component (protein-dominant, GI ~0), and (3) the dilution effect of the chicken-to-rice ratio in a standard serving. The composite dish GI is estimated at approximately 45 (low), consistent with mixed protein-carbohydrate dishes documented in Atkinson et al. 2021. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per serving in grams (6.8 g per 100g × 350g serving = 23.8g carbohydrate) / 100 = 10.7 g GL per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (650 mg per 100 g) from light and dark soy sauce used in the braising master stock
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid soya sauce chicken rice due to its high sodium content of 650 mg per 100 g; a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 2,275 mg sodium, which exceeds the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive patients, and the dish should be consumed only occasionally with sauce minimised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load per serving (GL ~10.7) and moderate sugar content (5.2 g per 100 g) from rock sugar in the braising liquid
type2_diabetes.guidance: Soya sauce chicken rice has a low glycemic index (~45) and moderate glycemic load per serving (~10.7), making it a relatively suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled; however, the sugar added via rock sugar in the braising stock and the sodium content from soy sauce warrant monitoring, and patients should request reduced sauce and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (650 mg per 100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~3.5 g per 100 g) from chicken skin and braising fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume soya sauce chicken rice cautiously due to its high sodium content and moderate saturated fat from chicken skin; removing the skin before eating, requesting less braising sauce, and limiting portion size to 200–250 g can meaningfully reduce sodium and saturated fat intake, and the dish's high protein content (18.2 g per 100 g) and low carbohydrate load are otherwise compatible with a heart-healthy dietary pattern when sodium is managed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soya Sauce Chicken Rice (豉油鸡饭 / 豉油雞飯; Malay: Nasi Ayam Kicap) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish of Cantonese origin, consisting of tender braised chicken served over steamed jasmine rice and drizzled with a glossy, sweet-savoury soy-based braising sauce. The chicken is first gently poached and then braised in a 'master stock' (lu shui / 滷水) composed of light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, rock sugar, ginger, garlic, star anise, and cinnamon, which imparts a deep mahogany colour and a complex sweet-savoury-herbal flavour profile. The meat is succulent and tender, with a slightly sticky, lacquered skin. The dish is a staple at Singaporean Chinese roasted meat stalls (siu mei stalls) and is commonly eaten at lunch and dinner. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-rich (18.2 g per 100 g) and moderate in fat (12.5 g per 100 g), with a relatively low carbohydrate content (6.8 g per 100 g) for a rice dish, reflecting the high chicken-to-rice ratio. The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (650 mg per 100 g), largely attributable to the soy sauce in the braising liquid, making it unsuitable for individuals on strict low-sodium diets. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), and the glycemic load per standard 350 g serving is approximately 10.7, making it a relatively moderate-GI meal choice. The dish is halal-certified at most Singapore hawker stalls and is dairy-free. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. Key allergens include soy and gluten from soy sauce.

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients are drawn from HPB Singapore Foodo records for soya sauce chicken rice and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for braised chicken and cooked jasmine rice. The match is adapted rather than exact because the HPB entry covers the composite dish and the precise recipe formulation (chicken-to-rice ratio, sauce volume) varies by hawker stall. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy braised chicken data. GI is an analogous estimate, not a directly measured value. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.5 g per 100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for braised chicken (USDA #05064) applied to the composite dish; no direct HPB measurement for saturated fat in soya sauce chicken rice was available as of 2026-04-16. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, cholesterol, and moisture data for this composite hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for steamed white rice and the dilution effect of the protein-dominant chicken component; no direct GI measurement for soya sauce chicken rice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (45) × available carbohydrate per serving (6.8 g per 100 g × 350 g = 23.8 g) / 100 = 10.7 g. 5. The input record description referred to 'Soya Sauce Chicken Wings' but the cr_id, food_name, and food_group clearly identify this as 'Soya Sauce Chicken Rice'; the description has been corrected to reflect the rice dish. 6. The input aliases referenced chicken wings (豉油雞翼 / 豉油鸡翅); these have been replaced with correct aliases for the rice dish (豉油鸡饭 / 豉油雞飯). 7. The input portions array contained two identical duplicate entries; deduplicated to a single default serving of 350 g reflecting a standard hawker plate. 8. The dietary tag 'plant_based' in the input has been removed as this dish contains chicken and is not plant-based. 9. FoodEx2 code A0CKM (braised chicken meat dish) is used as the closest defensible approximate match; no dedicated FoodEx2 code for soya sauce chicken rice exists.
